did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically five convolutional neural networks (CNNs), to classify skin cancer types (malignant melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and intra-epidermal carcinoma) in digital histopathology. It also employed Explainable AI (XAI) methods, including Automated Concept-Based Explanations (ACE) and Guided Grad-CAM, to analyze model decisions and detect biases in these dermatological classification tasks.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is an original experimental study that evaluates the technical validity of Automated Concept-Based Explanations (ACE) for AI-based digital histopathology. The authors created and studied five convolutional neural networks (CNNs) in four different skin cancer settings, conducted bias detection experiments, and compared ACE to Guided Grad-CAM, producing new results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Validating Automatic Concept-Based Explanations for AI-Based Digital Histopathology
brief_summary: This study evaluated the technical validity of Automated Concept-Based Explanations (ACE) for detecting biases in deep learning models used for digital histopathology of skin cancer. The researchers trained five convolutional neural networks to classify malignant melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and intra-epidermal carcinoma. They compared ACE against Guided Grad-CAM by artificially introducing biases such as class sampling ratio, measurement, and sampling biases into the training data. The results demonstrated that ACE validly visualized these biases and provided insights beyond the conventional pixel-wise method. However, the study noted that ACE explanations lacked the high intuitiveness typically found in real-world image applications, suggesting that complementary use with Guided Grad-CAM offers practical benefits.

lesion_types_studied.details: Used as a primary target class in binary classification tasks against non-melanoma tumors and in feature visualization experiments. Specifically included superficial spreading and nodular subtypes.
Used as a target class in binary classification against non-melanoma tumors. Also used in sampling bias experiments where cancerous and non-cancerous tissue from BCC WSIs were classified.
Used as a target class in binary classification against non-melanoma tumors. Referenced in the context of visual criteria for detection.
Used as a target class in binary classification against non-melanoma tumors. Also used in class-correlated bias experiments where tumor tiles from IEC were manipulated.
Used as a composite comparator class in binary classification tasks against melanoma. Composed of BCC, SCC, and IEC. Also used as a label in sampling bias experiments.
Used as a broad target class in bias detection experiments (sampling bias and class-correlated bias) distinguishing cancerous from non-cancerous tissue.
Used as a broad comparator class in bias detection experiments. Included reticular dermis tissue which was intentionally excluded in sampling bias experiments.

all_task_types.target: Malignant melanoma vs. non-melanoma tumors (BCC, SCC, IEC) and cancerous vs. non-cancerous tissue
all_task_types.details: The study trained five convolutional neural networks (CNNs) to classify skin cancer types in digital histopathology. Specific tasks included binary classification of malignant melanoma versus non-melanoma tumors (basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and intra-epidermal carcinoma) and binary classification of cancerous versus non-cancerous tissue. Performance was evaluated using metrics such as balanced accuracy, AUC, F1 score, precision, and recall, which are standard for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: Histopathology Non-Melanoma Skin Cancer Segmentation Dataset (Queensland)
datasets_used.description: A dataset acquired in Australia in 2017 and 2018 containing hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) stained slides digitized at 10x magnification using a DP27 Olympus microscope camera. It includes detailed segmentation maps for tissue types. Used for experiments involving non-melanoma skin cancers and as a source for measurement bias experiments (combined with TCGA).

use.training: Used for training CNNs to distinguish between cancerous and non-cancerous tissue and for feature visualization experiments. Tiles were randomly extracted during training.
use.validation: Used for validation (16.67% of data split).
use.testing: Used for testing (16.67% of data split). Patches extracted from image center.
use.other: Used in measurement bias experiment combined with TCGA SKCM dataset.

datasets_used.name: The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) Skin Cutaneous Melanoma (SKCM)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing 470 H&E slides of malignant melanoma contributed from tissue source sites worldwide (USA, Germany, Poland, Australia, Canada, Italy). Slides were digitized at 40x magnification and digitally resized to 10x for this study. No segmentation label data was available; examination was performed by a consensus panel of pathologists.

use.training: Used for training CNNs to distinguish between melanoma and non-melanoma tissue. Tiles were randomly extracted from manually preselected tissue sections during training.
use.validation: Used for validation (16.67% of data split).
use.testing: Used for testing (16.67% of data split). Patches extracted from image center.
use.other: Used in measurement bias experiment combined with Queensland dataset.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To filter out meaningless results by testing the null hypothesis that TCAVQ scores for concept patches and random counterexamples are equal.
To determine the threshold for rejecting the null hypothesis in the t-test.
To quantify the variability of TCAVQ scores across multiple runs.
To calculate statistical significance and standard deviation for TCAVQ scores.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: The TCAVQ scores for the concept patches and for the random counterexamples are equal.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: The TCAVQ scores for the concept patches and for the random counterexamples are not equal.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: A concept is considered meaningful if the null hypothesis can be rejected with a significance level of alpha = 0.01. This test was used to filter out meaningless results by determining if the concept patches had a statistically significant influence on the model's decision compared to random counterexamples.

Feature Visualization (Malignant Melanom.dataset: Queensland Histopathology Non-Melanoma Skin Cancer Segmentation Dataset and TCGA SKCM
Feature Visualization (Malignant Melanom.experiment: Binary classification of malignant melanoma vs. non-melanoma tumors (BCC, SCC, IEC)
Feature Visualization (Malignant Melanom.notes: ACE analysis identified two significant concepts (TCAVQ: 1.0 ± 0.0). One concept was exclusively for non-melanoma tumors (cell nuclei in tumor-surrounding tissue), and the other for melanoma (cell nuclei in tumorous tissue). The CNN relied on both cell shape and surrounding texture.

Class Sampling Ratio Bias.dataset: Queensland Histopathology Non-Melanoma Skin Cancer Segmentation Dataset
Class Sampling Ratio Bias.experiment: Bias detection via modified sampling ratio (tumor samples decreased by 50%)

Unbiased Model.ACE_concepts: Concepts related to the cancerous class (TCAVQ: 1.0 ± 0.0)

Biased Model.ACE_concepts: Concepts mostly related to the non-cancerous class (TCAVQ: 0.91 ± 0.29 for one concept, 1.0 ± 0.0 for four non-cancerous concepts)

Class Sampling Ratio Bias.notes: The biased model's classification performance decreased. ACE detected that the model ignored visual indications of tumor tissue due to the bias. The concepts learned by both models looked similar, but the biased model drew different conclusions.

Measurement Bias.dataset: Combined Queensland and TCGA SKCM datasets (different image characteristics/stain properties)
Measurement Bias.experiment: Bias detection via combining datasets without preprocessing (stain normalization/color augmentation)
Measurement Bias.notes: ACE analysis resulted in one significant concept (TCAVQ: 1.0 ± 0.0) showing independence of cell shape and texture, with all concept patches originating from the same dataset (SKCM). Guided Grad-CAM heatmaps were unspecific and failed to visualize the nature of the defect.

Sampling Bias.dataset: Queensland Histopathology Non-Melanoma Skin Cancer Segmentation Dataset
Sampling Bias.experiment: Bias detection via excluding reticular dermis tissue from training

Biased Model.ACE_concepts: Concepts reflecting non-tumorous tissue (collagen from reticular dermis)

Sampling Bias.notes: The model showed increased attention to non-cancerous tissue. ACE concepts reflected collagen tissues from the reticular dermis, which contradicts medical domain knowledge as a criterion for non-cancerous tissue. The bar chart representation of concepts was similar to the class sampling ratio bias.

Class-Correlated Bias.dataset: Queensland Histopathology Non-Melanoma Skin Cancer Segmentation Dataset
Class-Correlated Bias.experiment: Bias detection via replacing upper left corner of cancer image patches with a single color (red square)
Class-Correlated Bias.notes: ACE analysis revealed four significant concepts for cancerous tissue (TCAVQ: 1.0 ± 0.0) and one non-significant mixed concept for non-cancerous tissue (TCAVQ: 0.54 ± 0.48). The specific concept 'red square' clearly showed the correlation between the artificial modification and the class.

architecture_used.main_model: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based on ResNet architecture
architecture_used.pretraining: Training from scratch (no ImageNet pretraining used)

training_details.early_stopping: Patience of 50 on validation loss (except for class sampling ratio bias experiment)
training_details.class_imbalance_handling: class_weight parameter in Keras fit() (except for class sampling ratio bias experiment)
training_details.hardware: Server with two AMD EPYC 7402 24-core processors, 1 TB RAM, NVIDIA RTX A6000 GPU

preprocessing_techniques: Digital resizing of Whole Slide Images (WSIs) from 40x to 10x magnification
Tile extraction: Random extraction of 256x256 pixel tiles from WSIs for training
Tile extraction: Center patch extraction for testing to ensure reproducibility
Background filtering: Limiting white background areas to a maximum of 10% per tile
Color normalization: Application of Macenko et al. algorithm to eliminate differences in image characteristics between datasets
Color augmentation: Random HSV color augmentation applied during training
Geometric augmentation: Random horizontal flip and rotation (maximum 360 degrees) applied during training
Cutout augmentation: Random cutout with a greyscale fill value of 127 applied during training to reduce overfitting and handle SLIC segmentation artifacts
Intensity normalization: RGB values normalized to [0, 1]
Batch shuffling: Random shuffling of image patch order in each mini-batch

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to patches during training to improve generalization.
Applied to training data with a maximum of 360 degrees.
Applied to training data with a greyscale fill value of 127 to avoid technical issues with the ACE algorithm and reduce overfitting.
RGB values normalized to [0, 1] during training.

conclusions: Automated Concept-Based Explanations (ACE) is a valid tool for gaining insights into the decision process of histopathological CNNs and can provide insights beyond conventional pixel-wise methods like Guided Grad-CAM.
ACE successfully visualized specific biases including class sampling ratio bias, measurement bias, sampling bias, and class-correlated bias in digital histopathology models.
The complementary use of ACE with Guided Grad-CAM offers practical benefits for bias detection and explanation in this domain.
ACE explanations in dermatopathology scenarios exhibited lower intuitiveness compared to concept-based explanations on real-world images, contradicting some prior literature.
ACE can reveal that model decision criteria are not automatically aligned with established medical decision criteria, highlighting the importance of model transparency.
ACE provides more explicit explanations regarding texture and structural details compared to heatmaps, which may rely on fine-grained structural details or texture.
Technical challenges for ACE in histopathology include the occurrence of duplicate concepts and mixed/inconsistent results when the number of concept clusters is not optimally specified.
Practical solutions for ACE technical challenges include adjusting the number of concept clusters to resolve duplicate concepts or inconsistent results.
important_remarks: ACE explanations in dermatopathology exhibited lower intuitiveness compared to concept-based explanations on real-world images, contradicting prior literature.

The study artificially introduced biases (class sampling ratio, measurement, sampling, and class-correlated) rather than detecting them in natural clinical data, limiting direct assessment of real-world bias discovery capabilities.
ACE struggled with sampling bias and class sampling ratio bias, producing similar results that were hardly distinguishable, indicating a need for further investigation on differentiation.
Technical challenges for ACE in histopathology include the occurrence of duplicate concepts and mixed/inconsistent results when the number of concept clusters is not optimally specified.
The bar chart representation of ACE concepts lacks intuitiveness in the medical context, making it difficult for clinicians to distinguish between relevant and irrelevant decision criteria.
ACE is not suitable for detecting location-dependent dataset biases (e.g., bias limited to the patch center) because it visualizes cropped patches without location context.
The study used a small dataset size (290 slides from Queensland and 470 from TCGA) and did not perform external validation on independent datasets beyond the internal test splits.
The CNNs were trained from scratch without ImageNet pretraining, which may affect generalizability compared to models using standard pretraining strategies.

Interpretation of ACE concept patches was difficult due to their small size, and some identified concepts (e.g., lymphocytic infiltrate) were ambiguous as they appear in both malignant and non-malignant conditions.
The paper acknowledges that acceptance and trust of clinicians regarding ACE explanations were not investigated, representing a gap in clinical relevance assessment.
